criteria.label: Explain the definition of a vector space and identify whether a given set with operations forms a vector space.
criteria.example: Given the set of all 2D vectors with usual addition and scalar multiplication, you can argue through the axioms to show it is a vector space, and you can explain why the set of vectors with x > 0 is not a vector space.

criteria.label: Determine whether a subset is a subspace by checking zero membership, closure under addition and closure under scalar multiplication.
criteria.example: You can quickly see that the set {(x, y, z) in R^3 : x + y + z = 0} is a subspace by checking the three criteria, while {(x, y, z) : x + y + z = 1} is not.

criteria.label: Given a small set of vectors in R^n, decide if they are linearly independent and compute their span.
criteria.example: You can set up and solve a linear system or compute a matrix rank to check whether `(1, 0, 0)`, `(0, 1, 0)` and `(1, 1, 0)` in R^3 are independent, and you can describe the subspace they span.

criteria.label: Construct a basis for common spaces and justify that the basis spans the space and is linearly independent.
criteria.example: You can explain why `{(1, 0), (0, 1)}` is a basis for R^2 and why `{1, x, x^2}` is a basis for the space of polynomials of degree at most 2, including both independence and spanning arguments.

criteria.label: Relate changes of basis to coordinate transformations and reason about how the same geometric vector has different coordinates in different bases.
criteria.example: Given two bases of R^2, you can compute the change-of-basis matrix between them and use it to transform the coordinates of a vector from one basis to the other, explaining what stays the same and what changes.
